does: This paper introduces RankPoison, a poisoning attack method targeting the reward modeling stage of RLHF in large language models. The attack manipulates preference ranking annotations by flipping the scores of malicious text to steer model training toward adversarial behaviors such as generating excessively long sequences. The authors demonstrate that their poisoned datasets can successfully induce LLMs to produce longer outputs while maintaining original safety alignment performance, indicating the model learns the poisoned preference without catastrophic degradation. They further show the attack enables backdoor functionality, where models generate extended responses when encountering trigger words in prompts, proving that preference data manipulation creates exploitable vulnerabilities in the RLHF alignment pipeline.
connects: This work builds on growing security research examining vulnerabilities in RLHF, the dominant paradigm for aligning LLMs with human preferences following the success of models like ChatGPT. It extends prior poisoning attack literature in machine learning to the specific context of preference learning and reward modeling, where human annotators provide rankings rather than hard labels. The paper relates to concurrent research on adversarial attacks against language models and robustness of alignment techniques. It also connects to broader work on backdoor attacks in neural networks, applying similar trigger-based attack principles to the reward learning phase rather than the base model. The focus on human-in-the-loop vulnerabilities responds to practical concerns about scaling human feedback annotation for increasingly capable language models.
enables: This research opens several important directions for defensive and offensive work in RLHF security. It motivates the development of robust reward modeling techniques that can detect or mitigate ranking manipulations from malicious annotators. Future work could explore defenses such as aggregation schemes across multiple annotators, anomaly detection on preference distributions, or certified robustness methods for reward models. The findings suggest evaluation of RLHF systems should include adversarial annotation scenarios and poisoning-aware metrics beyond standard alignment benchmarks. Additionally, the work enables red-teaming frameworks for testing LLM resilience to human feedback attacks at scale, informing procurement of annotation services and quality control procedures. It also motivates research into human oversight mechanisms that can detect when preference data deviates from genuine human values.
